column7:
Yeah, but you can only ruthlessly murder that young kid *once*. You can enslave that young kid, and his family, and his kids, and keep them enslaved for generations, using and abusing them at your will. While they're your slaves, you can brutalize them, beat them, starve them, rape them, or even poison them with nerve agent, just for fun. 

Honestly, given a choice between the two, I'd rather die choking on the nerve gas.

>ISIS is unequivocally a hate group. That's their only mission, and they proudly profess it. 

This is incorrect. They have about as much a claim to nationhood as any confederate does - ISIS (ISIL, the
Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant, whatever you want to call it) organized, got together, and proclaimed that they were a separate nation. They claimed territory and enforced it with violence, all of which is pretty much identical to how the Confederate States of America started off.

Also, while they definitely hate people of certain ethnicities and from certain countries, the Confederacy also hated from certain ethnicities and from certain countries, so I don't think you can use this to differentiate the two.

Sure, ISIL does terrible, horrible things to innocents in the name of their nation, but the Confederacy did, too.

column7: I am from NC and the majority of people I see getting married young are people from small rural towns who are raised in a strict Christian traditionalist mindset and never leave their small town and I guess I am being judgmental towards that.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Always wears a piece of vintage jewelry']

column7:
I'm from NC but am not a christian (was an atheist at the time I married, am now a pagan) and have traveled a fair bit. I got married right after my 21st birthday. 

1: I had been living with my husband for 2.5 years when we got married, not the longest dating period but not exactly short. We felt confident that marriage was the natural next step.

2: he is over a decade older than me, waiting until my 30s to marry would mean him waiting until his 40s or 50s to marry. Which didn't seem reasonable, especially since we wanted kids. 

3: I come from a family where everyone married young, often as teenagers. My 21 seems old compared to my mom's 17 and my grandma's 14. 

4: It worked out really well for us anyway because we found out I was pregnant with our oldest daughter five days after our wedding.
 
5: It doubly works out because I ended up having my kids young, before I developed endometriosis which cost me an ovary and covered my fallopion tubes in cysts and generally reduced the chances of me ever having kids again. If I had waited until my 30s I would have been fucked because I would already have pretty much been sterile. 

6: Maybe not the best reason to marry, but the money we got at the wedding really came through for us at a time that we really needed it (see surprise baby) and let us do some things to better prepare. 

7. I also started developing some medical issues around the time of the marriage (also the surprise baby) and getting married allowed me to get on my husband's health insurance. Which really came through for me and probably saved my life in the long run.
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7: @all-gymnast, I get where you're coming from, but it's not fair to generalize like that. My cousin from a small Czech town married young too, but they're super happy and it's not because of some strict mindset. They just knew they were ready.
column19: ['Active', 'Too young to vote, but interested in social issues', 'Enjoys tinkering with gadgets and creating digital art']
